Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Left Orthodox, averaging 83.9 kph (up to 92.6).
  • Typical length is 4-5m (their good length); median 4.5 m.
  • Stock ball is full on the stumps, turning away, drifting in, hitting the stumps (28% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 96% of the time against right-handers.
  • Turns it 3.9° on average; 21% of balls turn ≥5°.
  • Across an over he holds a fairly consistent length.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Full (107 wkts, 1.8% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: Wide of 6th.
  • Takes 32% of wickets pitching full on the stumps, round the wicket.
  • Beats the bat 3.3% of tracked balls (false-shot 8.7%).
  • Gets you LBW more than most — 26% of his wickets (1.3× the typical rate for this type).
  • 29% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, square leg: short leg, point: silly.
  • Very repeatable with his length — using your feet to change his length is more effective than waiting for a loose ball.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching full on the stumps (22% of runs).
  • Away from full, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak square/through the off side (38%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes good length outside off (economy 3.8, beats the bat only 8%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — full on the stumps, turning away, drifting in, hitting the stumps (28% of deliveries, economy 2.35). Minor variations: full in the channel (19%) and full outside off (13%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %

a good length on the stumps ▶turning away, drifting in10%2.651610%
a good length in the channel ▶turning away, drifting in9%2.901610%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

48% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 19 balls; most runs go to the off side (38%); the drive hurts most (125 fours/sixes at 1.8 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the leg side (56%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it full on the stumps most often (26%); rarely drops short (1%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched full on the stumps, but strikes most often pitching wide outside off.

Speed & Spells

He holds his pace across spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Consistent with his length (length spread ±1.1 m; more repeatable than 66% of spin bowlers); holds a steady length right through the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.
And tends to strike with a straighter ball after working across the batter (36% straighter than the ball before).
Across 167 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.

Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 83% of left-arm spin); releases about 84 cm from the middle stump; varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 79% of left-arm spin). Comes wider round the wicket (57 cm over vs 84 cm round).

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
